teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with no table lead, I want to start the trick cheaply, keep the initiative if possible, and avoid revealing high cards early. A low spade lead keeps my stronger cards (4-6 of spades) for later tricks while forcing opponents to show some strength.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 unknown cards and no public actions have occurred, so I have almost no information about their hand. They could hold trumps, high cards, or continuations in spades. I will assume they are likely to have some playable cards but cannot rely on a specific suit or rank.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each have 3 unknown cards. With trump rank 7 and no cards played, they may hold trumps, high non-trump cards, or low cards similar to mine. I believe they are more likely than my partner to try to beat a low lead, but this remains highly uncertain.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades is the lowest legal play. It minimizes card value expended, keeps 4-6 of spades for later tricks, and does not split a potential run. If opponents beat it, I learn about their strength cheaply; if they cannot, I keep the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest spade. Playing it first wastes my best non-trump card on an opening lead where opponents with trumps or higher ranks will likely win anyway, leaving me with weaker 3-5 cards afterward.
The 5 is still a relatively high card in my weak hand and is not needed to win an opening trick. Saving it for a later trick where it might actually take the lead is better than spending it now.

The 4 is a reasonable middle card, but the 3 is strictly lower and accomplishes the same goal of a cheap lead while preserving slightly more strength. There is no signaling benefit to leading the 4 over the 3.

risks: Any opponent with a 7-ranked card, trump, or higher non-trump can beat the 3 and take the lead.
If partner is weak in spades or out of the lead suit, they cannot support this trick.
I reveal that I have spades, though no specific strength.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card, I limit the information gained by opponents and preserve my 4-6 of spades for potential recaptures or endgame plays. I accept that the opening lead is unlikely to win outright.

notes: Opening lead with a very weak hand (3-6 of spades, all non-trump) favors a minimal, low-card lead to keep options open.
